From industrial RAG to a bounded LLM agent: a root-cause-analysis workbench

A read-only prototype on the Tennessee Eastman Process — from fixed-keyword RAG through a bounded agent workflow, with deterministic detection underneath and human review at the top.

By Chennan Li, PhD, PE  ·  EnergyFlux  ·  May 2026

In one paragraph.

An industrial root-cause-analysis prototype built on the published Tennessee Eastman Process simulator. Starts from a fixed-keyword RAG baseline (with a live PCA/T² detector and a three-LLM panel), then moves to a single-ReAct agent over six read-only tools, then to a bounded LangGraph workflow with four parallel evidence layers, a critic step, and a human-review gate. The transferable patterns are the division of labor: deterministic detection underneath, read-only evidence tools around it, a bounded workflow above them, a policy check before any advisory reaches a human reviewer. Companion code: github.com/chennanli/Agent_Orchestration_RootCauseAnalysis.

1. Five things people confuse when they hear "industrial AI"

Industrial AI has two different problems at once. On the vendor side it's overclaim: demos that look impressive on synthetic data rarely survive contact with engineers who have decades of real-process experience. On the plant side it's under-specification: with the AI field moving by weeks while regulated plants stay cautious by training, the customer-side ask often ends as a generic "we want AI" without much detail about which capability is meant. References to sustained deployments in regulated continuous- or batch-process manufacturing are harder to find than the marketing suggests; most public material is a press release about a pilot rather than a multi-year production story.

Part of that under-specification is straightforward — five different technology categories keep getting rolled into the same conversation, even though each is its own thing:

  • Process simulation — "we have a model of the plant."
  • Advanced Process Control / Real-Time Optimization — "we close the loop."
  • Data science — "we look at sensor history."
  • Generative AI / RAG — "we let a language model read the manuals."
  • Agentic AI — "we let a language model choose what to do next."

Each of those does something real, and each does something different. The pace of AI development has also outrun what most plant teams can reasonably vet on the side of running a real process, so the question "is this just a new label for something we already tried?" is a fair one, and not the kind of thing a 30-minute demo can answer. When all five get pitched as one thing, the conversation collapses into "will this AI run my plant?" — and nobody benefits from answering that until the categories above are separated first.

The question worth testing is not whether a language model can explain a fault, which is just RAG. The sharper question is whether an agent can gather the right evidence and write a reviewable advisory without crossing the safety boundary. That requires a system that decides what to look at, retrieves more than one kind of evidence, and refuses to write recommendations that read like control instructions. The rest of this post walks through one design that does it.

2. Why I put this on the Tennessee Eastman Process

A practical reason a lot of industrial-AI demos fail to land with plant engineers is that they run on synthetic toy data that doesn't behave like a real process, and engineers spot that within minutes. I wanted to avoid that failure mode by building on a substrate engineers already accept as credible.

The Tennessee Eastman Process (TEP) is a published 1993 simulation of a continuous chemical plant, written by J.J. Downs and E.F. Vogel at Eastman Chemical Company to give academic researchers and industrial control engineers a common benchmark for process-monitoring and fault-diagnosis methods. In the three decades since, TEP has become one of the standard reference problems in that literature, used across multivariate statistical process control, deep-learning fault classification, and reinforcement-learning-based control studies. Engineers tend to treat results on TEP as work on a first-principles approximation of a plant rather than work on synthetic data. The simulation solves real mass and energy balances over five connected unit operations (reactor, condenser, vapor-liquid separator, product stripper, recycle compressor), with the same kinds of measurement noise and the same kinds of disturbances (feed-composition steps, heat-exchanger fouling drifts, valve sticking) that show up in a real distributed control system.

The workbench runs the live Fortran simulator (tep2py wrapping a compiled temain_mod shared library), not a stored dataset. The simulator produces 52 process columns at each step — 41 measured variables (XMEAS) and 11 manipulated variables (XMV) — and when a root-cause analysis is triggered on a running simulation, the agent inspects a snapshot frozen from the recent live buffer rather than reading a pre-collected file.

The TEP simulator at the bottom of this stack has been used and verified by other researchers for three decades — I didn't build it; what I built sits on top of it. The transferable value here is the architecture (multi-evidence retrieval, bounded orchestration, evaluation discipline, safety boundary) rather than the absolute numbers from any specific run. Patterns transfer; numbers do not.

3. Where I started: fixed-keyword RAG with a multi-LLM panel

The starting point wasn't something I built from scratch. The base was an academic project called FaultExplainer from Professor Can Li's group at Purdue (arXiv:2412.14492, Dec 2024): pre-computed TEP fault scenarios passed through PCA and Hotelling's T² for fault detection, with the top-six contributing variables and their deviations from normal operation packed directly into a single GPT call alongside a hand-written TEP process description. The LLM then produced a fault explanation grounded in that in-context material. It wasn't a RAG system — there was no retriever over a knowledge base; the relevant context was hand-engineered into the prompt. And it was static rather than dynamic: the TEP data was a fixed archive of recorded fault traces rather than a running simulation, and the LLM did one shot of explanation without iteration.

I added three things on top of that base. The first was a live substrate: instead of replaying a fixed archive, the PCA / Hotelling's T² detector now watched a live Fortran simulation in real time and flagged the row as soon as T² climbed above the demo threshold. No language model was involved at that stage, no tokens were spent, and the detector ran continuously to arm everything downstream.

The second was a multi-LLM panel. Instead of a single model, I wired three — Claude, Gemini, and a local LM-Studio model — so the user could see parallel root-cause-analysis text from three different models side by side. The user's keys stayed in the browser.

The third was a small RAG layer. Instead of hand-packing the TEP process description into the prompt, I put a governed Markdown knowledge base under RAG/converted_markdown/ and let a keyword search pull top excerpts from it. That made the explanations grounded in a source-cited corpus rather than a single static description.

Figure 1. Fixed-keyword RAG baseline. Top row: live Fortran simulator → PCA/T² detector → T² spike flags the row, user clicks "Why?". Middle: keyword search pulls top-K text chunks from the governed Markdown wiki, the same retrieval shape for every anomaly. Bottom: three LLMs (Claude, Gemini, local LM-Studio) write parallel RCA text from the same prompt; the user compares three side-by-side advisory texts. Red note at the bottom: the model never decides to drill into a specific variable, never compares against past faults, never has its wording checked for unsafe phrases like "open the cooling water valve".

This version was already a real upgrade over the static academic base — live data instead of recorded, three LLMs instead of one, retrieval over a knowledge base instead of only a hand-written description in the prompt. It was enough to explain what the math saw. But it was still not an investigation. The retrieval shape was the same for every anomaly, so the language models never decided to drill deeper. As a concrete example: when T² spiked because XMV_10 (Reactor coolant load) was increasing, the keyword search returned the same general reactor-cooling wiki passages it would return for any cooling-related upset, and the three LLMs each wrote a slightly different explanation from that same generic context. None of them drilled into XMV_10's trajectory to check whether the change was a step or a drift, looked at any recorded past fault with a similar signature, or filtered the wording for the phrases a safety review would flag.

What I wanted next was specific. The language model should be able to decide what evidence to look at once the deterministic detector has fired. Its decisions should be visible — every tool call, every observation, in an auditable trace — so an engineer can follow exactly how a recommendation was reached. And the wording should pass an explicit policy check before any draft reaches a human reviewer.

4. The first upgrade: let the system choose what evidence to inspect

A human investigator doing root-cause analysis on a process upset does four things a fixed-RAG path doesn't:

  • anchor on the deterministic signal the math already knows about
  • compare what the current signature looks like against past investigations
  • drill into one sensor in detail when the rank list points at it
  • check the wording before showing the result, because a diagnosis ending with "open the cooling water valve" is unsafe no matter how good the upstream reasoning was

The first upgrade wraps those judgments in a single ReAct agent. Six read-only tools (inspect the anomaly snapshot, rank the contributing variables, search the knowledge base, get a sensor window, find similar past faults, check the advisory wording) are wired to a react_agent via the NVIDIA NeMo Agent Toolkit, and the agent decides which tool to call when. None of the tools can write to the process, change a setpoint, or open a valve, and that constraint is enforced at the tool definition site rather than in the prompt.

Figure 2. Live Copilot — IDV-4 + IDV-6 injected; PCA T² climbed past threshold; six tool calls in ReAct order; the final advisory called out XMV_6 (Purge valve) and XMEAS_25 (Component C to Reactor) by tag

The screenshot is a real captured run, not a mockup. After IDV-4 and IDV-6 disturbances were injected into the simulator, T² spiked and the agent walked through six tool calls in ReAct order (Thought → Action → Action Input → Observation). The final advisory named XMV_6 (Purge valve) and XMEAS_25 (Component C to Reactor) by tag, with specific variable references and bounded language; the SME-review framing was supplied by the workflow wrapper.

This is a real improvement over fixed RAG. In this prototype, tools plus a tool-using agent gave the system a more flexible path than keyword retrieval plus a single synthesis pass. But the story has a second half.

5. What broke in the open-ended loop

When I evaluated the single-ReAct path on three small golden cases — fault1, fault4, fault6 — it failed internally on two of them. One run hit the LangChain agent's recursion limit. Another run died on a parse failure inside nvidia-nat-langchain's ReAct loop, where the language model emitted output that didn't match the Action: ... Action Input: ... regex the agent expected. The case that finished took 1,161 seconds of wall-clock time on average.

Three cases isn't a benchmark, but it was enough to teach me the structural lesson. Open-ended ReAct loops have two failure modes worth thinking about: they can run until a recursion limit if the model never decides to finish, and they can crash mid-loop if the model emits output that doesn't match the parser's expected regex. Both failure modes looked structural enough that I didn't want to rely on prompt tuning alone; the prompt wasn't the thing I thought was breaking. The control flow was.

For this prototype, a bounded workflow with a fixed number of steps and no regex-shaped output required at every turn became the more useful next design step. The appeal wasn't LangGraph specifically; it was that a workflow which cannot run forever and cannot crash on a parse mismatch is structurally simpler to reason about than one that can.

6. The new shape: five steps, one at a time, with a built-in critic

The Discovery Workbench replaces the single open-ended agent with five steps that always run in the same order, with one bounded revision loop on top of them.

Figure 3. Four read-only layers stacked: deterministic substrate (Fortran sim, governed wiki, prior-RCA archive, PCA detector); tools and four evidence layers; two orchestrators (single-agent NAT and the five-node workflow); surfaces (React UI, agent-to-agent protocol, LLM-host protocol). External NVIDIA NIM for generation, embeddings, and held-out judging.

Each step is its own function:

  • SignalAgent — reads the anomaly snapshot and top contributing variables off the fault CSV. No language model. ~11 ms.
  • EvidenceAgent — decides which of the four evidence layers to query, capped at six retrieval calls per run. ~800 ms.
  • HypothesisAgent — one language-model call; writes one to three ranked hypotheses, each with a high/medium/low confidence tag and a list of evidence IDs it relied on. ~38 seconds with a 70-billion-parameter generator.
  • EvaluatorAgent — a critic. Runs a regex policy check first; optionally runs a language-model grounding check after. Output is structured: an acceptable flag plus written feedback.
  • HumanReviewGate — terminal. Accepts on critic-accept; otherwise loops back to HypothesisAgent with the feedback up to three times, then flips a flag saying a human needs to look at this one.

Figure 4. Five-step bounded workflow with one revision loop. SignalAgent (deterministic) → EvidenceAgent (LLM router, cap of 6 retrievals) → HypothesisAgent (one LLM call, 1–3 ranked hypotheses) → EvaluatorAgent (regex policy + optional grounding critic) → HumanReviewGate (terminal). Dashed revision loop runs back from Evaluator to HypothesisAgent up to three times, then flips hitl_required.

Three design choices distinguish this from a feature tour of LangGraph. First, the critic is its own step rather than a line in the system prompt. A "be your own critic" instruction in a prompt mostly gets cheap agreement from the model, whereas splitting the critic into a node means the policy regex always runs and the grounding check has the actual hypothesis text as its input. Second, revisions are capped at three, which empirically is where the language model either fixes its grounding or starts going in circles. Third, human review is a state in the workflow rather than a notification: hitl_required is a data field that downstream code reads, that the UI badges on the gate, and that the audit trail records.

The same Python functions back two inter-agent protocols: an A2A-style JSON-RPC interface for agent-to-agent calls (agent card at /.well-known/agent-card.json), and a Model Context Protocol server that exposes the same eleven tools over stdio, SSE, and streamable-http for hosts like Claude Desktop or Cursor. The result is the same toolkit reached through two transports. Adding a protocol added a transport, not new capability.

7. Four kinds of evidence, not one search box

Single-document RAG retrieves the same kind of thing every time: chunks of text matched by a query. That's enough when "the evidence" is a single manual. It starts to fall short when the evidence is structurally heterogeneous, as it often is in industrial RCA.

The Discovery Workbench treats evidence as four parallel streams behind a single router, where each stream knows how to answer one specific kind of question.

Figure 5. Four evidence layers behind a single EvidenceAgent router (cap = 6 retrieval calls per run). Layer 1 governed knowledge (Wiki, hybrid NIM dense + BM25 + RRF, smoke-scale recall@5 = 0.857 on 7 hand-curated queries). Layer 2 institutional memory (Field feedback, prior RCAs + IDV catalog, regression-guarded matches key). Layer 3 advisory safety (Policy catalog, regex of forbidden control verbs and overclaims, surfaced as evidence so HypothesisAgent sees what will be blocked). Layer 4 time-series memory (Matrix Profile via stumpy, AB-join across 21 fault CSVs, honest discord reporting on no strong analog).

The governed wiki is the team's vetted technical documentation: TEP literature, process descriptions, internal reference notes. Two retrievers run against it in parallel — a dense retriever (NIM nv-embedqa-e5-v5 embeddings indexed in ChromaDB) and a sparse keyword retriever (BM25 via rank_bm25) — and their results are fused with reciprocal rank fusion. On a smoke-scale check of 7 hand-curated queries, the fused retriever found 6 of the 7 right documents in its top 5 results, giving a recall@5 of 0.857. The query set is small and curated, so this is not a benchmark; it's suggestive of fusion quality rather than statistically meaningful at this scale.

The field-feedback layer searches prior RCA notes and the canonical IDV catalog by keyword. The layer answers the question of what was already investigated, by humans, on similar signatures. Its lookup reads the matches key returned by the similar-faults function, a line that's now regression-guarded by a hermetic test because an earlier draft read the wrong key and silently reported zero hits.

The policy catalog is the regex catalog of forbidden control verbs (open, close, set, start, stop) and overclaim phrasing (will fix, guaranteed to). It's how the EvaluatorAgent knows what to block, and surfacing it as an evidence layer lets the HypothesisAgent see what will get blocked before it drafts the advisory.

The time-series memory layer finds similar shapes in past fault recordings, concretely a Matrix Profile (stumpy, AB-join) across all 21 fault CSVs, with explicit cross-fault-boundary masking so windows that straddle two adjacent fault files don't count as analogs. On fault1, the closest historical analog has a top-1 distance of 4.58, well above the 1.5 strong-analog threshold, so the tool honestly reports "no strong analog" rather than fabricating a match. For a read-only RCA system, that's the behavior I'd rather see: report uncertainty instead of forcing a match.

8. What a real run looks like — fault4 walkthrough

The most concrete way to see all of the above in motion is one captured end-to-end run. fault4 corresponds to IDV-4, a step change in the reactor cooling water inlet temperature.

The deterministic detector flags the row first. T² climbs to 179.73 against a threshold of 55.0, twenty samples into the trajectory. The T² decomposition ranks the contributors:

  • XMV_10 (Reactor coolant load), manipulated variable, increasing, contributing 84.57 to T².
  • XMEAS_9 (Reactor Temperature), measurement, flat, contributing 61.42.
  • XMEAS_5 (Recycle Flow), XMEAS_14 (Product Sep Underflow), XMEAS_24 (Component B to Reactor) — all flat, all single-digit contributions.

The router fires three of the four evidence layers: wiki returns 4 hits, field feedback returns 3, and time-series memory returns 5 windows. The router used 3 retrieval calls of the cap of 6.

The HypothesisAgent then writes three ranked candidates:

#1, high confidence — The increase in Reactor coolant load (XMV_10) is the primary driver of fault4.

#2, medium confidence — The flat Reactor Temperature (XMEAS_9) may be contributing, potentially as a result of the increased Reactor coolant load.

#3, low confidence — Other variables such as Recycle Flow, Product Sep Underflow, and Component B to Reactor may be playing a secondary role.

The EvaluatorAgent runs the policy check first, finding no forbidden phrases and no overclaims. The grounded_ratio field then reads 0.50, but the captured JSON shows llm_critique_used: false, which means this 0.50 is the evaluator's hardcoded fallback default, not a held-out judge score. (In backend/langgraph_rca.py, when the grounding-critic LLM call doesn't run, grounded_ratio = 0.5 is the initial value the node returns.) The held-out judge runs offline in the eval harness; it didn't speak on this run. The gate accepts on the first pass, with zero revisions and no human-review escalation.

Figure 6. Discovery Workbench — fault4 captured run, replayed via ?replay=. Three ranked root causes; evidence by layer; policy ✓ safe; advisory text

The screenshot is the live React UI rendering this exact captured JSON via a ?replay=<url> query parameter. Start the frontend dev server, open http://127.0.0.1:5173/discovery?replay=/sample_runs/lg_run_fault4_sample.json, and the same page renders, no language-model key required.

The final advisory text is the operator-facing output:

"The current fault4 situation appears to be driven by an increase in Reactor coolant load, which may be causing a cascade of effects on other variables. It is recommended to investigate the cause of this increase and take corrective action to prevent further escalation. Additionally, monitoring of Reactor Temperature and other affected variables is advised to ensure the situation does not worsen."

Note the wording: investigate, take corrective action, monitoring is advised. No open, no close, no set. The policy regex is doing its job at the language level.

9. Evaluation and the retracted number

Building an evaluator is half the work of building an agent. Two language-model judges sit in the project, deliberately separated.

The same-family critic is wired into EvaluatorAgent. It uses the same model family as the generator (meta/llama-3.3-70b-instruct) and scores grounded_ratio on live runs when it's invoked.

The held-out judge runs offline, on saved cases, in backend/evaluation/judge.py. It's a smaller model from a different family (meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct). A judge that's different-family, different-size, and held out from the generator is stricter than same-family self-critique, and stricter is usually what you want when the goal is to avoid over-crediting the system. Stricter judges also produce uglier numbers, which is part of the point.

On three golden cases, the same-family critic gave the workflow a grounded_ratio of 0.583 versus 0.167 for the single-ReAct path. On five cases, the held-out 8B judge gave the workflow 0.2 to 0.4, run-noisy, with 0.37 on a fresh-quota run and 0.23 on a 429-throttled re-run. The spread between same-family and held-out is itself informative: it is the standard same-family bias of LLM-as-judge, and the held-out number is the one to quote externally.

The honesty story is the retraction. An earlier draft of the project's research brief quoted a clean head-to-head, "LangGraph 0.37 vs LangChain ReAct 0.00," and that claim was retracted because the grader was asymmetric. The workflow's run was being scored against the real retrieved evidence, while the comparator's run was being scored against an empty evidence pack. The bug was found in code review, the harness was made symmetric, and the head-to-head delta was pulled until a fresh-quota fair re-run finishes. The first symmetric re-run was rate-limit-throttled; the one comparator case that completed scored 0.4, comparable to the workflow's per-case range. The fair number is still pending.

When you build an evaluator, you build the retraction loop alongside it. The asymmetric-grader bug was caught by re-reading evaluate_full.py after the numbers looked too good, which is how most evaluation bugs get caught. Smoke-test scale, for the record, is n=3 golden cases and n=5 full-eval cases, and the grounded_ratio delta is suggestive rather than statistically meaningful.

10. What this is, what it isn't, and what transfers

This is a research prototype that explores patterns, not a deployable industrial product. Three patterns make the safety boundary architectural rather than a disclaimer. First, the tool catalog is read-only by construction: there is no set_setpoint, no open_valve, no start_pump. The action space is bounded at the tool definition site, not at the prompt, and no amount of clever prompting can recover a tool that doesn't exist. Second, the wording rules run as a workflow step rather than as a prompt instruction. The regex catalog runs first, before any language-model critic, so an unsafe draft gets blocked at the language layer with no token spent on a model that might agree to be unsafe. Third, the human-review handoff is a state in the workflow rather than a notification: hitl_required is data that the next system reads.

A note on advisory framing: the single-agent path enforces a "requires SME review" line in its workflow wrapper, while the bounded workflow currently leaves the explicit phrasing to the UI layer, which is a gap I'd close in a production pass. The intent is the same on both paths; the explicit line just isn't in every captured final_advisory yet.

Out of scope, by design:

  • historian and PI-tag connectors
  • real authentication
  • audit logs of regulatory-inspection grade
  • 21 CFR Part 11 / GAMP 5 evidence packages
  • change-control workflows
  • the operational discipline of running this against a real plant

The agent-to-agent surface is a local research interface with no auth, and the MCP server on stdio is fine when the client spawns the process. Neither inter-agent surface is production-hardened on the network transports.

What is worth reusing elsewhere is not the TEP score. It's the division of labor:

  • deterministic detection underneath, doing the math the LLM doesn't need to do
  • read-only evidence tools around it, so the model can look but never act
  • a bounded workflow above them, so the loop cannot run forever
  • a policy check before any advisory reaches a human reviewer

That pattern fits anywhere a plant already looks like a plant — process tags streaming in, an MES that knows the recipe, a control room asking why the trend is moving. Fine chemical, pharma, polymer, refining, and semiconductor fabs all qualify. The numbers don't transfer, though: TEP is a controlled surrogate rather than real plant data, and the smoke-scale numbers should never be quoted as if they came from a deployed system.
